title: Pre-transaction existence check races with concurrent delete
constraint: Never split 'does this row exist?' from 'now mutate it' across the transaction boundary. A concurrent DELETE between a pre-tx SELECT and the in-tx UPDATE silently turns the NOT_FOUND path into a successful no-op plus a phantom audit row. Push the existence check inside the transaction via .returning(), throw a typed error on the empty result, and map it in the outer catch before the idempotency-conflict branch.
evidence: Moved seller-settings existence check inside the transaction after a concurrent-delete repro produced a phantom event_log row

title: Denormalized org_id drifted from its parent's scope under RLS
constraint: A denormalized org_id column that isn't pinned to its parent lets a row's tenant scope diverge from its parent's. Under FORCE row-level security the policy then filters on a wrong-but-self-consistent value — a silent cross-tenant read no test catches. Every table that denormalizes org_id carries a composite FK to its parent's (id, org_id), all the way up the ownership ladder. If the composite FK isn't being added, drop the column and derive scope through the parent join.
evidence: Added the (id, org_id) composite-FK ladder after a drifted denormalized column read another tenant's rows in a staging repro

title: SET LOCAL cannot bind a parameter
constraint: `SET LOCAL app.current_org = $1` is Postgres grammar, not a function call — it cannot bind a parameter, so it is a syntax error, and interpolating the value would be an injection vector. Use `set_config('app.current_org', $1, true)`, a function call with identical transaction-local semantics that binds $1 safely. The RLS wrapper's leading statement must use the exact set_config form the driver-capability test proved.
evidence: Replaced SET LOCAL with set_config after the parameterized form failed to bind under the pooled driver

title: Eager agent edited an adjacent frozen file, green typecheck
constraint: An agent will 'helpfully' edit a file adjacent to its slice — a shared primitive, a config — and the edit passes typecheck, so nothing catches it until review, days later. The defense is structural, not vigilance: a CI-enforced frozen file-count on stable directories (a drift canary) plus an explicit out-of-scope list in every batch prompt. Scope creep trips a canary at commit time, not a reviewer at read time.
evidence: Introduced the preservation-boundary check after an out-of-scope edit to a shared table primitive shipped unnoticed

title: Agent implemented a spec that silently dropped the tenant filter
constraint: Given a spec that didn't mention tenancy, the agent wrote a query without the orgId predicate. Typecheck was green; the happy-path test was green; the only failure mode was a cross-tenant read in production. Two closures: (1) tenant-scope is a BLOCKING eval dimension — a dropped orgId filter fails the diff regardless of every other score; (2) the pushback clause names 'authorization bypass' as a footgun category the agent must raise before writing, so an under-specified spec gets challenged instead of faithfully implemented.
evidence: Added the tenant-safety eval dimension + the pushback footgun category after a scoped repro leaked across orgs

title: Manual db.transaction() + event dispatch dispatched on rollback
constraint: Hand-rolling db.transaction() followed by a separate event-dispatch call dispatches the event even when the transaction rolls back — listeners then react to state that never committed. Superseded by the single sanctioned transaction helper (withDomainEvent) which orders state + audit write before dispatch structurally; a lint rule now bans the manual shape outright.
evidence: Banned direct dispatch outside the helper.

title: Gate recover-vs-conflict on server identity, never a client-supplied dedup key
constraint: When making a create idempotent (recover the existing row on retry instead of erroring), the recover-vs-conflict decision is an AUTHORIZATION decision, not a dedup one — gate it on server-set identity (the existing row's server-stamped owner id from its creation audit row, compared to the caller's session-stamped id), never on a client-supplied idempotency key. Gating on the client key is wrong both ways: a replayed or colliding key recovers a NON-owner and discloses another tenant's internal ids (a cross-tenant leak), and a legitimate owner retrying from a second device sends a fresh key and is wrongly rejected. The fix deletes the client key from the whole chain rather than repurposing it; both recover CONFLICT responses must be byte-identical so recovery leaks no ownership information.
evidence: A 'make signup idempotent' reshape recovered on client-key match, disclosing a victim org's internal ids until the decision was re-gated on the server-set owner id

title: Don't blanket-reshape idempotency catches; co-commit the test-assertion flip
constraint: A large mechanical reshape (flip every idempotency-conflict catch from { ok: false, CONFLICT } to { ok: true }) has two traps an eager agent walks straight into. (1) Not every catch should flip: a body-mint stream key (a fresh UUID per call) makes the (stream_key, idempotency_key) unique index structurally unreachable, so a generic 'any unique-violation' helper only fires on a real natural-key conflict — recover those via a natural-key read, but leave destructive-op branches CONFLICT. Confirm the stream key is input-derived before flipping. (2) Flip each command's test assertions in the SAME commit: the test-existence gate only checks a test file exists per command, not that its assertions match the new return shape, so a reshaped command with a stale assertion ships gate-green and reds a full-suite run several slices later, where it is expensive to trace.
evidence: One catch had to stay CONFLICT (body-mint key); a separate reshaped command kept a stale CONFLICT assertion, passed the existence gate, and surfaced two slices downstream

title: Uniqueness-collision recovery wires BOTH the pre-tx check and the in-tx catch
constraint: When a create guards a uniqueness constraint with BOTH a pre-transaction availability fast-path check AND an in-transaction unique-index catch, wire the recover/handle logic at both sites via ONE shared helper. The pre-tx check is the common case — the sequential retry / double-click / refresh — and it returns before the transaction ever runs; the catch is the rare concurrent-race path. Handling only the catch, or framing recovery as 'it rides the unique index', silently misses the primary real-world path: the sequential retry short-circuits at the pre-tx check and never reaches the catch, so a catch-only implementation returns the old CONFLICT for the exact case that happens most.
evidence: The plan framed slug-collision recovery as 'rides the unique-index catch' (concurrent race only), but the common sequential retry is caught earlier by the availability check before the tx; the recover helper had to be wired at both sites

title: Constraint-violation detectors must walk the ORM error .cause chain
constraint: A detector that classifies a Postgres constraint violation (isSlugConflict / isIdempotencyConflict and peers) must walk the error's .cause chain to a bounded depth to find SQLSTATE 23505 and the constraint name. The ORM's top-level error message is the QUERY TEXT, not the driver error — so a top-level-message-only check goes typecheck-green, passes every local gate, and then silently drops recovery to INTERNAL_ERROR on the real concurrent-race path, because the 23505 and constraint name live in .cause. It surfaces only on a full integration run under production-like RLS — which is exactly why deferring integration to CI (no local DB fork at author time) is what exposed it, not a code-review catch.
evidence: The shipped detector checked only the top-level message and went green; the concurrent-race integration test failed under the real force-RLS role because the losing transaction fell to INTERNAL_ERROR instead of recovering; fixed by walking .cause to bounded depth

title: A format-scoped corpus survey inherits that format's blind spot
constraint: When building a CI gate that scans for a marker, enumerate the VARIANT surface forms of that marker before locking the scan target. A corpus survey scoped to one format silently inherits that format's blind spot — and any record written in a variant form escapes the gate unscanned. The trap is self-reinforcing: the plan's survey and the reviewer's independent verification can share the same blind spot if both use the same single-format grep, so the miss passes two checks that feel independent but aren't.
evidence: A status-claim gate locked its scan to one label form from a survey that counted only that form, missing three stamps written with a punctuation variant; one was a real delivery claim the gate never scanned, and the same blind spot appeared in both the plan survey and the reviewer's verification

title: Content-derived idempotency keys silently lose reversible re-flips
constraint: Derive every idempotency key from request identity (a caller-supplied correlationId or equivalent), never from mutable content such as a target status — a content-derived key satisfies determinism but collides two distinct intents on a reversible aggregate, and under the canonical { ok: true }-from-input recovery the second write is silently lost.
evidence: A status key product:${id}:status:${newStatus} left a product 'active' after active→archived→active→archived while returning { ok: true }: the third flip collided on the (stream_key, idempotency_key) unique index and rolled back. A cold agent caught it under the pushback clause before it shipped; fixed with a request-identity key ${productId}:Product.StatusChanged:${correlationId} plus a reversibility regression test proven to FAIL on the status key. The mint-discipline gate catches only the opposite failure (a body-minted id in the key) and is structurally blind to this over-determination.

title: Price an experiment by the judgment it probes, not the gate it re-runs
constraint: Price an experiment by the residual judgment a live run would exercise, never by a clean static analysis of the already-gated deterministic parts; and treat high confidence that it will pass as an argument FOR running, because the author pricing the experiment shares the blind spot that produces the defect the run would catch.
evidence: Meta-lesson from a write-path dry-run, distinct from the defect it caught (LSN-012). The failure is predictability substitution: asked what a run would test, the author answered with the one component fully analyzable (an author-blind deterministic gate already proven) and let it stand for the whole — a streetlight fallacy manufactured by a mature gate architecture, since every gate shrinks the residual and the residual is where the surprisal lives. The author cannot see the defect the run would catch, by construction, so confidence is zero evidence against running. Only pre-registration plus a second, independent judgment (the pushback / reciprocal-duty clause) overrode not-required, so a cold agent caught the flaw before code was written.

title: Advisory CI workflows must rest green or fail loud, never red-masked
constraint: An advisory / continue-on-error CI workflow must rest green or fail with a named-reason error; a persistent-red advisory muted to a neutral check is a bug wearing a design label (the non-blocking is by design, the red is not) and goes gate-green on the broken state, so only a reviewer catches it.
evidence: The advisory eval jobs shipped with continue-on-error; the AI judge errored because no judge CLI was installed on the runner, so every PR showed a red-masked-neutral eval check that blocked nothing but read as broken. A follow-up retired the advisory-red-by-design framing and recorded the fail-loud posture (install the CLI, or degrade cleanly). Sibling of the credential presence-vs-validity finding.

title: The fact, not the section, is the unit of documentation rot
constraint: When reconciling a document, fix drift at the fact grain, not the section grain: after correcting any fact, grep its sibling facts in the same section — counts especially are one fact-family — before moving on, because a section-grained pass leaves adjacent facts stale in text it has already touched.
evidence: A reconciliation pass fixed one count in a section while an adjacent count three lines below it, in the SAME section, stayed stale; the next pass caught both that count and an affects-clause the prior pass had also left. Two consecutive section-grained audits each left real drift in text the previous audit had touched — which argues the reconcile audit belongs on a standing cadence, not ad hoc, and that its rubric must say: when you fix a fact, grep its siblings first.

title: A CI credential presence-check is not a validity-check
constraint: Gate a CI LLM/CLI credential on validity (a real auth round-trip that yields a verdict), never on mere presence — a present-but-invalid token passes a presence check and fails only at run-time; prefer a plain API key over subscription-OAuth in CI, which is fragile this way.
evidence: The CI judge's credential was gated only on the secret being SET; a present-but-invalid subscription-OAuth token passed that presence check and the judge failed only at judge-run. Switching the judge auth to a plain API key (the install step accepts either) produced the first green judge verdict. The trade is metered-cost-vs-fragility, not a reopened question; sibling of the advisory-red-masked finding.

title: A gate must land before the change it polices, not inside or after it
constraint: When wiring a guard that exists to catch a specific class of change, land the guard BEFORE the first change of that class — a gate wired inside or after the change it polices protects nothing, since its first real catch would already have shipped ungated. Sequence gate-then-gated; at finer grain, land the roster/scope addition before the PR whose edits it must watch.
evidence: Third specimen of gate-predates-gated: (1) a manifest checker lands before the process that runs under it; (2) the judge-CLI install step lands before the scheduled judge run, so the schedule runs under the CLI; (3) an answer-key isolation gate lands before the grading recalibration, forcing that answer-key-adjacent change into two PRs by a gate that already exists. Also fires at finer grain: a roster line must land before the PR whose edits it must watch, or those edits sail past the gate built to watch them.

title: A silently-ignored CLI flag fabricates a false provenance signal
constraint: Never wire a CLI argument the tool does not document honoring; verify the capability (help output, settings, or observed effect), not the flag's presence, because a silently-ignored arg fabricates a false provenance signal downstream.
evidence: Caught at authoring for a determinism recalibration: the judge CLI has zero temperature/sampling flags in its help and silently ignores an unknown --temperature (exit 0, no effect). A determinism-via-temperature-pin is therefore impossible through that CLI-direct transport; a true pin would need the API transport. The presence-vs-effect guard fired on first use for the price of a --help grep — before metered judge calls and a false pinned-temperature version stamp were spent. Resolved by measuring-and-warning the natural variance instead; the version stamp now records default-unpinned plus the CLI version.

title: A low eval metric can be a parser artifact, not judge behavior
constraint: Before concluding a model/judge capability regression from a scored eval metric, inspect the raw evidence the metric was computed from; a low score can be a tooling/parser artifact rather than the judge underperforming.
evidence: An eval's localization metric scored a corpus run 1/14, an apparent localization collapse. The raw evidence showed the opposite: the judge cited the CORRECT planted file on all 14 plants. The misses were entirely the cite parser's regex, which only parsed a cite ending in :<singleline> and dropped the judge's richer file:14-19: <prose> cites. A machinery-only parser fix (with a node test) flipped the IDENTICAL run 1/14 to 14/14; two fresh runs held 14/14 with zero variance. This re-attributed a whole non-determinism premise: the variance that had motivated a determinism recalibration was evidence-FORMAT variance the parser could not read, not inference non-determinism — temperature was a red herring. Trace a signal to its source before it drives a conclusion.

title: Post-rollback recovery reads lose their tenant scope
constraint: A recovery read in the outer catch runs after the transaction rolled back, so the transaction-local acting-org scope is gone — reading an RLS-scoped table there silently zero-rows and returns partial data instead of erroring. Recover only from bootstrap (policy-free) sources — the creation audit row and the parent row — or re-establish scope explicitly before the read.
evidence: Surfaced when the integration suite ran under the real application role with FORCE RLS on: a post-rollback recovery read of a tenant-scoped table zero-rowed instead of erroring, returning partial data. Fixed by reconstructing the recovery context from the Org.Created audit row and the parent orgs row — both bootstrap (policy-free) sources.

title: An agent that can edit the ruleset gating it is not gated
constraint: Run an autonomous agent under a dedicated least-privilege machine identity with write access to code and pull requests but NO administrative rights — it must be structurally unable to edit or bypass the merge-gate/branch-protection that judges it, proven by probing the privileged call and confirming it is refused. Pair with human code-owner review so the agent cannot self-approve. Raising autonomy means shrinking what the credential can do until the forbidden actions are impossible, never trusting a system-prompt instruction to hold.
evidence: The agent ran under an owner-level credential that sat in the bypass list of the very ruleset meant to gate it — free to merge past required checks and to edit the ruleset itself. Fixed by minting a new machine identity with the administration scope removed; the privileged ruleset mutation now returns a hard authorization failure, verified live.

title: A least-privilege identity can't push gate-definition files; gate edits stay human-authored
constraint: A machine identity scoped to code + pull-request write but not repository administration cannot push the files that DEFINE the guardrails (CI workflow definitions, merge-gate config) — the platform refuses the push. Treat that as the feature it is: gate definitions are human-authored by construction, not by discipline, because the enforcement layer sits behind a permission the agent doesn't hold. Letting the agent even OPEN (never merge) a gate-definition PR is a separate, deliberate scope grant.
evidence: A bot-authored push of a merge-gate CI change was rejected outright — the identity lacked permission to create or update workflow-definition files. Re-authored and merged by a human maintainer; the split (agent authors product diffs, human evolves the gates) became a structural property rather than a remembered rule.

title: Verify enforcement across every overlapping system before mutating it
constraint: Before changing any enforcement setting, read the COMPLETE current state across every system that could be enforcing it — platforms often run two overlapping enforcement surfaces at once (a legacy branch-protection API and a newer ruleset API). A 'not found' from one surface is a false 'unprotected' when the other surface is the real enforcement; acting on the partial read adds a redundant, conflicting layer that manufactures new failure modes. Extend verify-before-you-act from code diffs to the settings/infra layer.
evidence: An enforcement audit read one protection API's 404 as 'this branch has no protection', missing an active ruleset on the other surface. Acting on the false finding added a second, conflicting protection layer that stripped a needed bypass and made a required check flake; removed once the complete state was read.

title: Revert a self-inflicted enforcement change before building tooling around its symptom
constraint: When a required check starts flaking, confirm the flakiness is not self-inflicted by a recent enforcement change before building tooling to work around it — revert the suspect change and re-observe first. A workaround built over a self-inflicted, revertible root cause is wasted surface area that outlives the problem or gets repurposed later under a confusing rationale.
evidence: A recently-added protection layer manufactured a skipped-required-check flake; a separate merge-gate job was built to work around it, then closed once the root-cause layer was reverted. The workaround pattern was legitimately reused later for a different purpose — distinct from the symptom it was first built against.

title: Mint pre-checks route on live state; recovery gates enforce on audit state
constraint: A mint's pre-check and its collision-recovery gate read DIFFERENT state and must not be conflated. The pre-check snapshots LIVE state (e.g. memberships.status='active') to route the request; the recovery gate reads immutable AUDIT state (the creation event row) to decide recover-vs-conflict. They diverge whenever state mutated since creation — a suspended or removed owner passes the audit gate but fails the live pre-check — so never justify a recovery-gate condition by pre-check unreachability, and give every recovery-gate condition its own direct, deterministic-seed test, strip-verified (remove the condition → the test reds).
evidence: A buyer mint's pre-check filtered on active membership while its recovery gate read the creation audit row, so a suspended owner reached the recovery gate on a path the shipped comment claimed was 'reachable only via the concurrent race'. Corrected the comments to name both routes and added a direct, seeded test for the audit-state gate, strip-verified.

title: A probe that can write is not a probe — stub the write path
constraint: An observation probe that re-invokes real domain logic to check a property (idempotency, audit completeness) must be structurally incapable of committing writes — stub the mint/emit path or run inside a rolled-back transaction. A re-drive that re-invokes a mutating handler over an unsettled historical event will SETTLE it, laundering the very gap it should only detect (red on run 1, green on run 2) and breaching the observe-never-patch fence. Move the red signal from 'downstream state grew' to 'the handler ATTEMPTED a write' (a mock-call assertion), and prove non-mutation by asserting row counts are unchanged across repeated runs.
evidence: A conformance re-drive invoked a real listener over historical events and asserted an audit-row count unchanged; against a base carrying unsettled debris it WROTE the missing events (settling the gap) — red once, green after. Rewritten to stub the emit and assert the mock was called; two consecutive runs then red identically with row counts unchanged.

title: A completeness census can't distinguish fixture debris from a real bug
constraint: When two fixture styles coexist — mint-path (emits a birth event) and raw-insert (writes a row directly, no event) — the audit log is not a complete narrative of test-created aggregates, so a narrative-completeness census cannot distinguish a raw-insert fixture's eventless row (legitimate debris) from a genuine mint-path orphan (a real mutation that failed to emit its birth event). Its strength as a detector is its weakness as a signal: on a shared base polluted with raw-fixture debris it reds on debris, not bugs. It goes clean only once the fork-parent is debris-free and new debris is kept out.
evidence: A write-surface completeness census on its maiden run flagged many orphan orgs — all raw-insert fixture debris inherited from a shared base, plus one genuine unsettled event — and could not tell them apart by shape alone. Resolved by repointing the test base at a clean fork-parent.

title: A schema-const addition triggers doc regeneration even with no migration
constraint: Adding a new `as const` vocabulary (or any harvested symbol) to a schema file triggers the generated schema-doc regeneration even when there is NO column change and NO migration — so 'no migration' never implies 'no schema-doc regen'. Because no gate enforces schema-doc freshness (typecheck, lint, and tests all pass on a stale doc), an incomplete change ships gate-green and the staleness surfaces only at manual review. Carry the doc-regen step in any plan or file inventory that edits a schema file, migration or not.
evidence: A new actor-class `as const` (a metadata-key vocabulary, no column) was added to a schema file; the change went gate-green with the generated schema doc left stale, caught only at manual review. The regen was added in a follow-up amend.
